name: Loki
whyHere: There is Precursor work in the Valley that nobody there understands, and you understand it, and that is dangerous for everyone including you.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

turns.summary: Loki arrives in Pressureholt at the Intake Plaza on a gray-green morning — the water crisis visible in the river itself.
turns.narration:
Pressureholt wakes the way a machine wakes — in sequence. The gas-lamps on the lower tier click off one by one as the morning light climbs the stepped roofline, each extinguishing on a timer someone wound forty years ago and nobody has dared reset. The smell is oil and river-stone and, this morning, something faintly off beneath both. The Echo River runs gray-green where it passes under the Eastern Intake, and the Enginewrights along the quay are gathered around a sampling fixture, speaking in careful voices.

You are standing at the edge of the Intake Plaza — cobblestones fitted so precisely they have not shifted in a generation — with the early-spring chill pressing down your collar. Your Traveler's Pack sits square on your back. Cy rides your left shoulder, lens irising at intervals, its too-many legs distributing its small weight with characteristic precision. It has already, at some point between sleep and waking, tightened the left strap buckle on your pack. You did not ask it to.

"The intake baffle shows particulate variance of eleven-point-three above baseline," Cy announces, for the third time this morning, in its clipped antique register. It is unclear whether it expects a response. Down on the quay, one of the Enginewrights — a broad-shouldered woman in a leather apron — glances up from her sampling kit. She clocks you, then the brass creature on your shoulder, and her expression does the thing Enginewright expressions do: it catalogs the problem you might represent.

The plaza is otherwise alive with the early business of the city. A wright's apprentice hurries past carrying a disassembled clock-movement in both hands, expression suggesting a man who has made a significant mistake. Two older wrights converse near the posted notices board, their voices too low to catch from here. One of the notices is fresh — the ink still dark — and it bears the seal of the city's maintenance authority. You are new here, and nobody has fixed you yet, which means they are simply waiting to understand what, exactly, is broken.

turns.player: Approach the wright on the quay and get a closer look at the sampling fixture — something in the particulate reading doesn't sit right
turns.summary: Loki reads the sampling fixture's particulate trap and identifies an anomalous ring-residue consistent with nanite substrate output — and Vessa Torquen notes that Cy seems to recognize it.
turns.narration:
You cross the plaza at a measured pace, boots finding the fitted cobblestones with the easy familiarity of someone who grew up around precision work. The broad-shouldered wright on the quay doesn't stand when you approach — she just shifts her weight and lets you into the view of the fixture without a word, the way a craftsperson does when they recognize another one.

The sampling fixture is a column of brass and glass about the height of your hip, with four graduated vials drawing live flow from the intake pipe. The readings are mechanical, analog, written in a script of notched dials and float-weights. Three of the four vials are measuring within expected variance. The fourth — the bottom-weight particulate trap — is wrong in a specific way. The gray-green color is a surface reading, and everyone here is watching it. But the trap has caught something finer: a flocculated residue settled in a ring at the vial's base, too regular to be sediment, the particle size too uniform for natural mineral suspension. You've seen that pattern before. Not in water. In a substrate output from a contained nanite process.

Cy moves up beside your ankle without being invited. Its lens irises wide, then clicks back to narrow. "Particulate variance," it says, in its precise, slightly-antique voice, "eleven-point-three above baseline. The ring-form is consistent with a regulated deposition event." It pauses. "This unit has logged this pattern. Classification is not available to this unit at this time."

The wright looks down at Cy, then up at you. "Your mechanism knows what that is?" she asks. Her tone is not hostile — it is the tone of someone who has been looking at a problem for two days and has run out of guesses.

turns.player: Use 'Falescraft to convince them that Cy is actually a small gremlin that follows you around as a pet and has lots of opinions about things, but is most often not correct.  Then use "the wrong reality" to get her to give up a personal secret that relates to the pets she keeps.
turns.summary: Loki attempts to disguise Cy as a pet gremlin and extract a personal secret from Vessa — Cy immediately contradicts the story, and the dense lattice collapses the Working; Vessa is not fooled.
turns.narration:
You crouch slightly and let the Falsecraft settle into your voice — the practiced ease of someone telling a story they have told a hundred times. Cy is not a Precursor relic, you begin. Cy is a gremlin. A small, very opinionated gremlin who wandered into your pack three years ago and has never left. He has views on gear alignment, strong feelings about the angle of sunrise, and he is almost always wrong about everything, but he means well.

Cy's lens irises open to its full width. It regards you for exactly two seconds. Then it says, in its precise, antique cadence, 'That is incorrect. I have not been wrong since the fourth maintenance interval.' It does not appear to understand that it has just undercut the entire performance.

Vessa Torquen watches this exchange with the calm of a person who repairs things for a living and has therefore seen many things fail in real time. She does not look convinced. She looks like someone who has just watched a clock skip a tooth and is now quietly counting to see which hour it will miss. 'That,' she says, nodding at Cy, 'is not a gremlin.' She tilts her head, and there is something measuring in her eyes. 'And you are not a naturalist. So what are you, exactly, and what does your not-gremlin actually know about my intake readings?'

The dense lattice here is not helping — the Pressureholt substrate crowds the Working so completely that the seam in your story was visible almost before you finished telling it. The ring-form residue sits in the fourth vial, unexplained, and Vessa's attention has landed on you with the full weight of someone who fixes things until they understand them.

turns.player: Excuse yourself from Vessa's attention and walk over the the posted board - look around for any young attractive women to introduce yourself to along the way.
turns.summary: Loki excuses himself from Vessa and walks to the notices board, spotting a young wright in a rust-orange coat posting something to the lower rail.
turns.narration:
You give Vessa a small, honest nod — the kind that says 'you win this one' without quite admitting there was a contest — and step back from the sampling fixture. She watches you go with the same folded-arms patience she had when you arrived, and then returns her attention to the fourth vial.

Cy falls into step at its customary distance behind you, its many legs clicking a precise rhythm on the fitted cobblestones. You take the long way to the notices board, moving along the quay's edge where the morning foot traffic has started to thicken. A pair of apprentice wrights carry a tool chest between them, arguing quietly about a bracket. A gray-haired woman in a long work apron stands near the water's edge and stares at the river with an expression that has worry in it. Closer to the board, a young woman is pinning something to the lower rail — her coat is a deep rust-orange, sleeves rolled to the elbow despite the chill, dark hair pinned back with what appears to be a precision caliper. She has the look of someone in the middle of a task she is taking seriously.

The board itself is dense with postings. Most are maintenance schedules and duty rosters in the tidy, compressed hand that Pressureholt seems to favor. The fresh notice near the top still has dark ink, sealed with the maintenance authority stamp — whatever it says, it was posted this morning, before the lamps went out.

turns.player: Try to get a sense of what the young woman's tastes and wants are before you approach - make modifications to your affect and shift your hairstyle to get the best possible first impression... then introduce yourself while studying her notice very intently.
turns.summary: Loki reads the young wright before approaching, adjusts his manner, and introduces himself at the notices board — the dense lattice leaves a slight roughness in the working.
turns.narration:
You hang back for a moment near the quay railing and watch her work. She moves with the particular efficiency of someone who has been doing a routine task long enough that her attention has gone elsewhere — the notice goes up straight, the pin driven home with a single practiced push, and then she rocks back and reads it critically, as though checking someone else's work. Rust-orange coat, rolled sleeves, hands that are ink-stained at the right fingertip on the right hand — she writes things down for a living, or records them. The coat has three different patches on the left sleeve, each a different fabric, each applied neatly. A person who fixes what breaks rather than replacing it.

There is something else, too. The way she squared her shoulders when the two older wrights passed — a very small straightening, gone before they had turned the corner. She is junior here, or she feels herself to be, and she does not want it visible. That is useful to know.

You run a hand through your hair — a small thing, unremarkable to anyone watching — and let the Misdirect settle into the shape of you, shifting your affect from the measuring quiet you had at the sampling fixture toward something easier and more interested. Not performance. A different true thing. The lattice is dense this close to the intake works, and for a moment it presses back against the adjustment, crowding the signal with its own constant noise. You feel the seam of it — a slight roughness in the shift, like a coat that doesn't quite lie flat. You let it ride rather than force it.

Then you step up beside her and look at the notice, genuinely. The ink is still dark. It is a maintenance posting, three columns of careful handwriting, a seal from the district authority at the bottom. 'Loki,' you say, without looking away from the page. 'New to the plaza. That's your hand on the posting?'

turns.player: Ask her directly what she records here — lean into the junior-wright dynamic and treat her role as genuinely important.
turns.summary: Loki reads the hand-kept variance log at the notices board and learns it has diverged from the automated markers for eleven days.
turns.narration:
You lean in and read the notice she has just pinned — three columns of careful handwriting, every entry dated and cross-referenced. The title at the top reads: EASTERN INTAKE — PARTICULATE VARIANCE LOG, SPRING INTERVAL. Below it, a column of timestamps, a column of readings, and a narrow third column of notes in a smaller hand.

You say, with genuine interest, that this is thorough work. You say you have not seen a variance log kept by hand before — most places you have been through rely on the automated tally-markers. You ask her what the hand-log catches that the markers miss.

She turns from the board and looks at you properly for the first time. Her expression is cautious, the way a person is cautious when they expect to be dismissed and are not yet sure they won't be. She says the tally-markers record what they are calibrated to expect. She says her log records what is actually there. She adds, after a short pause, that those two things have not agreed for eleven days.

Cy, at your shoulder, irises its lens once. It says nothing. But the lamp it carries tilts a fraction toward the notice, the way it tilts toward things it finds significant.

turns.player: Offer to help her think through what that means back at her place... you've been around things like this your whole life.  Tell her you're also good with your hands and wink.
turns.summary: Loki offers Tessvel practical help with the diverging log — and a wink — and she names the time her posting ends.
turns.narration:
You let the silence after her last answer sit for a beat — the kind of silence that says you heard it, not just waited for your turn to speak. Then you close the variance log column with one finger and look at her directly.

"Eleven days is a long time for a divergence to go unaddressed," you say. "Someone should sit down with that log and actually work through what it means." You pause, and then add, easily, that you have been around things like this your whole life. Old mechanisms, old patterns. You know how to read what the instruments miss. You say this without boasting — as a plain fact, the way she states her numbers.

Then you let the corner of your mouth go, just slightly. You tell her you are also good with your hands. The wink is small and unhurried, the kind that exists and then is gone without demanding a response.

Tessvel Cairn holds your gaze for a moment longer than the conversation strictly requires. Then she looks back at the log, and you can see the precise, orderly part of her working through whether this is a useful offer or a distraction she does not have time for. She taps the lower edge of the notice once with her index finger — a habit, not a decision. When she looks back up, there is something in her expression that was not there before. Not warm exactly. Considering.

"My posting ends at the second bell," she says. She says it the way someone states a calibration reading — flat, exact, and leaving the interpretation to you.

turns.player: Ask what she does when her posting ends — keep the thread open without pressing it
turns.summary: Loki asks Tessvel what follows her posting — she names ten free minutes between the second and third bell, and leaves the rest unsaid.
turns.narration:
The second bell is not yet rung. The plaza holds its early-morning chill — the kind that settles into cobblestones and doesn't lift until midday, if the season agrees to cooperate.

You ask her, lightly, what she does when the posting ends. Not pressing. Just leaving a door open in the wall of the conversation.

Tessvel looks at the notice board for a moment before she answers. She does not look at you. She straightens one corner of the log she just pinned, even though it does not need it. Then she says that she usually writes up her notes from the morning and files them before the shift changes. She says it the way someone says a routine thing when they are deciding whether to say the less routine thing underneath it.

Then she glances sideways at you. "The filing takes about ten minutes," she says. "After that, I am unassigned until the third bell." She does not say anything else. She picks up her ink stylus and caps it, which is something to do with her hands.

Cy has moved without announcement to a cobblestone near your left boot. Its lens irises briefly toward Tessvel's hand-log, and then toward you. It does not speak. You get the distinct sense it is noting something in whatever passes for its records.

turns.player: Ok - it's a date then... can I meet you at your place? I'm new here.  It's important to see where you work to know how to best help you think through what you have here.
turns.summary: Loki proposes meeting Tessvel at her workstation — she gives him directions to the Pressure-Routing Annex and confirms the window between bells.
turns.narration:
You let the offer land easy, like it was always going to be said. You tell her it is a date, then — and you mean it exactly as much as you let it sound like you mean it, which is quite a lot. You say you are new here and that you would like to see where she works. You say it plainly: it helps to understand the place before you can help with the problem inside it.

Tessvel looks at you for a moment. Not calculating — more the way someone looks at a door they have just decided to open. She caps her stylus and slides it into her coat pocket, the one without a patch. She says her work station is in the Pressure-Routing Annex, east side of the main works, third gate left of the big wheel. She says this precisely, the way she writes dates. She adds that it is not impressive to look at — it is mostly shelving and a long table — but that the light is good in the mornings.

She picks up her log and tucks it under her arm. The second bell is still not rung. The gray-green water moves below the quay, and Cy clicks quietly at your heel, lens turned toward the river rather than the conversation, which is the closest thing to tact you have ever seen from it. Tessvel glances down at Cy — not worried, not unkind — and then looks back at you with something that might be the beginning of a question she has decided to save for later.

"Between the second and third bell," she says again, and this time it is not a schedule. It is a confirmation.

turns.player: Take her hand quckly and kiss it.  then tell her you'll meet her there. And you'll bring Cy for her to meet as well.
turns.summary: Loki kisses Tessvel's hand and confirms the meeting at the Pressure-Routing Annex — she agrees, and walks away at no particular hurry.
turns.narration:
You take her hand before the moment can thin out — quick, certain, the way you do everything that matters — and bring it to your lips. The back of her knuckles. A brief press, not a performance.

Tessvel goes very still. Not startled, exactly. More the way a well-calibrated instrument goes still when something unexpected registers on it, and it is deciding whether to log the reading.

You straighten and tell her you will meet her at the Annex between the bells. Then you add — easily, as though it is a small thing — that you will bring Cy. You say it with a slight tip of your head toward the brass-dark shape at your heel, its lens already irised toward her, lamp angled politely away.

Something moves in Tessvel's expression. Quick, and then controlled. She looks at Cy for a moment longer than she looks at most things. Then she says, carefully, that she will be there, and she tucks her log under her arm and turns toward the main works without looking back — though she walks, you notice, at no particular hurry.

turns.player: Watch her walk and take full detailed note off all the best of her features - what are her dimensions and attributes... how well does she move?  Watch her walk away and see if she looks back to see you watching.
turns.summary: Loki watches Tessvel walk away — she moves with efficient precision, pauses at the plaza's edge, and looks back once before she goes.
turns.narration:
Tessvel walks away at no particular hurry, and you watch her go with the careful attention you give to things worth understanding.

She moves well. Not a trained walk — nothing self-conscious about it — but efficient in the way that people who spend their days navigating tight machinery corridors learn to be. Her stride is even and unhurried, each foot placed with the same quiet precision she gives her handwriting. She is not tall, but she carries herself at her full height, which amounts to the same thing. The rust-orange coat falls to mid-thigh and she has her hands in its pockets now, the patched left sleeve catching the early light. Her hair — dark, pinned up with what looks like a spare tightening-rod rather than a proper pin — stays in place as she walks, though one piece at the nape of her neck has come loose and moves with her pace. Her frame is lean and capable-looking, the kind that comes from actual work rather than intention.

At the edge of the plaza, where the cobblestones meet the passage leading toward the main works, she pauses. She does not make a show of it. She simply stops, half-turns, and looks back once — a measured look, not a lingering one, the way you check a reading before you file it. Her eyes find you exactly where she left you. Then she turns and goes.

Cy's lens irises once beside your ankle, tracking her departure. It clicks quietly, twice, and says nothing.

turns.player: Head toward the Pressure-Routing Annex — third gate left of the big wheel — ahead of the second bell.
turns.summary: Loki arrives at the Pressure-Routing Annex ahead of Tessvel — three gates left of the big wheel, morning light across the long table, Cy pausing in the doorway with a click it does not explain.
turns.narration:
You leave the intake plaza as the morning chill begins to soften — just barely, the way early spring concedes warmth in small installments. Cy falls in beside you at its habitual distance, lamp swinging in a short arc, legs picking their way over the cobblestones with unnerving precision.

The main works are easy to find. The big wheel is visible from three streets over — a weathered iron circle, taller than two men, turning at a steady pace that suggests it has never once been asked to stop. You count the gates left of it: one, two, three. The third gate is narrower than the others, framed in dark timber with a pressure-gauge mounted at eye level, its needle sitting just inside the green.

Inside, the Pressure-Routing Annex is exactly what Tessvel described and nothing more: shelving, a long table, stacks of bound logs with dates inked on the spines. The morning light comes in from the east through a high, narrow window and lays itself across the table in a clean pale bar. The room smells of machine oil and paper. Cy stops in the doorway, lens irising slowly around the space. It makes a small sound — a single, measured click — and does not explain it.

Tessvel is not here yet. The second bell has not rung. You have arrived first, which means you have a moment to look at what she works with before she arrives to explain it.

turns.player: Ask Cy why it stopped... evaluate the space for privacy in case Tessvel wants to have some intimate moments.  Prepare to impress her with your technical knowhow, while you see if she wants to have intimate play... so where in the room would lend itself best for that...
turns.summary: Loki tried to read the Annex for privacy and found none — the room is bright, narrow, and open to the gate; Cy cited an anomalous resonance signature and would not explain it further.
turns.narration:
You ask Cy why it stopped.

Cy's lens irises once, slowly, and it says: "Anomalous resonance signature. Classification: pending. Recommend standby." Then it steps fully into the room and resumes walking as if nothing happened, its lamp swinging at its habitual angle. It will not say more. It never does.

You turn your attention to the room itself — trying to read it the way you'd read a mechanism, looking for the quiet corners, the angles where two people might exist without the rest of the world having opinions about it. The Annex does not cooperate. It is narrow, brightly lit from a high east-facing window that sends morning light in a clean bar straight down the center of the long table — illuminating everything, hiding nothing. The shelves run floor to ceiling on both sides. There is exactly one chair. The pressure-gauge at the door frame ticks softly, and anyone passing the third gate left of the big wheel can see straight in through the open doorway. You are standing in what amounts to a well-lit cabinet. The lattice here is dense — you can feel it the way you feel a crowd, all signal and no clarity — and when you try to get a quiet read on the space, the interference folds back on itself and tells you nothing useful. The room is functional. It is warm enough. It is exactly as private as a notice board.

Cy has already begun examining the pressure-gauge at the door frame, its legs planted with the careful stillness of something that has found an interesting problem.

turns.player: Use falsecraft on the room so that when Tessvel arrives, it is shrouded and private.
turns.summary: Loki wove a partial attention-deflection over the Annex doorway — not true privacy, but enough to keep passing eyes from settling.
turns.narration:
You reach for the lattice — and it's there, dense and humming, a crowd of signals that press back the moment you try to shape them. The Annex is saturated. Every gauge, every pipe-joint, every logged system in the works is breathing into the same substrate, and carving a quiet pocket out of it is like trying to whisper in a foundry.

You manage something. Not a shroud — the bright east window keeps its bar of light; the gate stays exactly as open as it was. But you nudge the attention-shape of the room, a light reorientation. Anyone passing the gate outside will find their eyes sliding from the doorway rather than settling on it. A passer-by in a hurry will not stop. Someone looking for this gate specifically will still find it. It is the difference between a closed door and a door painted the color of the wall beside it.

Cy watches you from the far end of the table. Its lens irises once. It does not comment, which for Cy is the loudest possible commentary.

The effect will hold for a while — until someone walks through it looking, or until the bell rings and the gate traffic thickens and the lattice noise overwhelms the seam. You have bought a measure of quiet, not a room.

turns.player: Examine the anomalous resonance signature Cy flagged — use See the Made Thing to read what it actually is before Tessvel arrives
turns.summary: Loki's attempt to read the anomalous resonance at the Annex doorway failed — the works' dense lattice-noise drowned the signal, leaving only an impression of something old, pre-Annex, unclassifiable.
turns.narration:
You crouch at the doorway where Cy stopped — the pressure-gauge to your left, the threshold itself unremarkable to the eye — and you reach through the lattice with See the Made Thing, trying to read whatever Cy flagged. The dense signal-noise of the works closes around you immediately. It is like trying to hear a single voice in a market at full cry. You find the doorway's material facts: timber, iron threshold-plate, the faint harmonic of the attention-deflection you laid there yourself. Below that, something else — a texture that doesn't belong to the room. But the lattice is too loud. The signal crowds the shape before you can hold it still enough to read. You get an impression only: old, not this building's old, older than the Annex by a long way, and then the surrounding noise rolls back over it and the reading collapses. You are left with a headache at the bridge of your nose and nothing you could write down.

Cy, from the far end of the table, clicks twice. Its lens is aimed at the doorway, not at you. It does not offer a translation.

The bell has not rung yet, but it will soon. The morning light is past its sharpest angle and has begun to spread, and the sounds from the main works outside have picked up — more footsteps, a pipe-joint being hammered somewhere two passages over. The partial attention-deflection at the door is already thin against that rising traffic.

turns.player: Step fully across the threshold and try again from inside — the lattice may read differently a half-pace deeper
turns.summary: Loki reads the threshold deeper — an old boundary, pre-Annex, pressed into the substrate like scar tissue; the second bell sounds.
turns.narration:
You step across the threshold and crouch on the inside of the doorway, where the timber meets the iron plate. The lattice here is still loud — the works breathe into every corner of this room — but you are past the outer ring of it now, and something shifts. The signal-noise does not quiet, but it layers differently, the way a crowd sounds different when you are in it rather than at its edge.

You reach again with See the Made Thing. This time you hold a shape before it dissolves. Old. That is the clearest word for it — old in a way that has nothing to do with the timber or the iron plate, which are new by comparison. The impression is of something laid down before the works were built, before the Annex was built, before whatever stood here before the Annex. It is not a signal. It is a record — the ghost of a function, pressed into the substrate so long ago that the substrate has grown around it the way scar tissue grows around wire.

You cannot read what the function was. The lattice-noise is still too thick for that. But the shape of it is circular — a boundary, not a mechanism. Something was meant to stop here, or to be stopped here. Cy's lens tracks toward you from the far end of the table, irises, and then looks away, as if it already knew what you would find and has no comment to add.

From outside, faint and clear, the second bell begins to sound through the works.

turns.player: Ask Cy — quietly, before she arrives — whether it has seen this pattern before in the old service-ways
turns.summary: Cy names the threshold-mark — a terminus-seal, pre-Transition, one of four beneath the valley, three intact — and Tessvel arrives as the second bell ends.
turns.narration:
You keep your voice low, the way you would in a library or a church. You ask Cy whether it has seen this pattern before — the circular impression, the boundary pressed into the substrate like an old scar.

Cy's lens irises once, slowly. It does not turn to look at you. It says, after a moment, in its careful antique phrasing, "The pattern is consistent with a terminus-seal. Origin: pre-Transition. Function: to hold a boundary against propagation. This maintenance unit has catalogued four such seals in the old service-ways beneath the valley." A pause. "Three are intact. One is not."

It does not say which one. It does not say where. It picks up its lamp and holds it at the angle it always holds it, and you are not sure whether the omission is intentional or simply the way Cy answers questions — everything accurate, nothing complete.

Outside, the second bell finishes its count. In the passage beyond the open gate, you hear the measured step of someone who walks like she is always on time.

turns.player: Say nothing yet — let her come to the table and start her own morning routine before you introduce yourself or your questions.
turns.summary: Tessvel arrives at the Annex on time, files her first log entries without looking up, and notices Loki with careful, cataloguing attention.
turns.narration:
Tessvel comes through the gate with the precise, unhurried step of someone who has timed this walk many times. She sets a leather satchel down on the table, buckles it open, and draws out a bound log — green cover, the spine inked in her own hand with a date range. She does not look up. She sets the log flat, opens it to a marked page, and draws a pen from inside her coat pocket. Then she fills in two lines without consulting anything, because she already knows what goes there.

Only then does she see you.

She does not startle. Her pen-hand stills. She looks at you the way she looked at Cy on the plaza — a careful, cataloguing attention, taking in the fact of you before deciding what category to put it in. Her gaze moves to Cy, then back. She says, "You are early," and it is not a complaint. It is a data point she is filing.

She glances at the open gate. The attention-deflection is still there, thinning at the edges — she does not appear to notice it, but she does not close the gate behind her either, leaving the passage open the way wrights do in a room where they expect to be accountable for what they are doing.

She pulls the single chair out for herself, then appears to remember she is not alone, and leaves it where it is — a small, deliberate correction. "There is only one chair," she says to you, without apology. "I can bring a second from the passage if you require it."

turns.player: Tell her you came to observer her... two primary axes... one, how proficient she is at her work  and two.. .how cuter she is while performing it.  Tell her you will write a report for either her supervisor, or her boyfriend... (indicating you are verifying she doesn't have one) upon request.
turns.summary: Loki opened with a two-axis compliment and a bureaucratic flirtation; Tessvel confirmed she has no boyfriend and that she also has no posted authorization for him — both pieces of information delivered with exactly the same precise voice.
turns.narration:
You let the silence hold for one more breath after her pen-hand stills. Then you smile — not the wide, performative kind, but the smaller one, the one that knows something. You say that you came to observe her on two axes. The first, you say, is professional — how proficient she is at her work. You pause just long enough that she might think that is the whole of it. The second, you say, is that she is remarkably cute while performing it, and you wanted that on record somewhere.

Her pen does not move. She is looking at you with the same measuring attention she gave the log entries, but the quality of it has changed — there is something behind her eyes now that is calculating a response and has not yet decided which one. A faint color has come into her face, just above the collar of the rust-orange coat.

You add, pleasantly, that you are prepared to file a report with her supervisor, or with her boyfriend — and here you let the word sit between you for a moment, open like a question you are pretending is not a question — upon request, whichever seems most applicable.

The color in her face deepens one shade. She says, very precisely, that she does not have a boyfriend. She says it the way she filled in those two log lines — from memory, because she already knows what goes there. Then she seems to hear herself say it and her chin comes up a degree, and she adds that she also does not generally allow observers in the Annex without a posted authorization. Her pen-hand is still not moving. She is waiting to see what you do with all of that.

turns.player: Let her know that you will stay or go upon her wish... and that you will either help her with the work first, or help her understand just how cute she is first... as she wishes. ;)
turns.summary: Loki offered to stay or go on Tessvel's terms — work first or flattery first, her choice. She answered the flattery part first, precisely, and did not ask him to leave. Then she put him to work on the eleven-day variance.
turns.narration:
You lean back just a little in the one chair, easy and unhurried, and you tell her — in the same tone you might use to offer directions — that you will stay or go entirely as she wishes. You gesture at the open log on the table. You say you are happy to help with the work first. Then you let the pause do its work. Or, you say, you can help her understand exactly how cute she is first. Entirely her preference. You say it with the smaller smile again, the one that is not showing off.

Tessvel's pen is still. She looks at you for a full three seconds. Then she sets the pen down with a small, deliberate click against the table, which you suspect is what she does when she needs her hands not to be holding something. She says, carefully, that she is going to answer the second part first. She says: she is aware of her competencies. She is also aware, she says, that you are sitting in an unposted observer's chair in a restricted annex, and that there is no form for this. She picks the pen back up.

Then — and this is the part that matters — she does not ask you to leave. She pulls the green-spine log a half-inch closer to her side of the table. She says, without looking up, that if you are going to be here, you may as well be useful. Her handwriting moves across the page in one clean line. She says: the variance in the hand-log against the tally-markers has been running eleven days. She says: nobody else has asked about it. Her voice is professionally even. But she said nobody else, and she said it with the faintest weight, the kind you would only catch if you were paying close attention.

turns.player: Tell her something true about the log variance, then ask her to unbutton the top button of her uniform.  Tell her that for every demonstration of cuteness you will use it to power your insights. use the wrong reality if that will asssist.
turns.summary: Loki offered truth and a touch of charm together; Tessvel named both halves precisely and declined neither — the variance is real, the flattery is filed, and nothing has moved yet.
turns.narration:
You lean forward, elbows on the table, and you tell her something true. You say the variance has been running eleven days and no senior wright has touched it. You say that means either nobody noticed, or nobody wanted to. You say she noticed. You say she kept a second log. You say that is not clerical work — that is engineering, and she knows it. All of that is true, and you deliver it plainly, because truth lands better than most things you could build. Then you pause. Then you say, with the small smile, that for every demonstration of her considerable charm she allows, you will pour the resulting inspiration directly into the problem in front of you. And you ask her — quiet, light, a question rather than a push — to unbutton the top button of her uniform.

Something shifts in the room. Not dramatically — Tessvel does not move. She looks at you for a long moment, and what happens behind her eyes is not warmth or offense. It is a very precise recalibration. She picks up her pen. She sets it down again. She says, evenly, that she has heard the first part before — that nobody noticed, or nobody wanted to. She says she does not know which yet either. Then she says that the second part, the button part, is a non-standard approach to intake-variance analysis, and she would like to know where exactly it appears in the methodology.

She is not asking you to leave. She is also not unbuttoning anything. She is looking at you the way someone looks at a number that does not divide cleanly — interested in what it means, not yet sure what to do with it. The Wrong Reality found nothing to grip. The lattice here is too dense, too literal. The fabric of the room did not bend. Tessvel herself is more resistant than the substrate — she felt the seam of it, you think, the small wrongness in the air, and she filed it as a question rather than accepting the frame you offered.

turns.player: Answer her question directly — tell her the methodology is yours and you will demonstrate it rigorously if she has the patience for independent research
turns.summary: Loki claimed the methodology as his own; Tessvel — steady, precise, and quietly considering — asked to see it applied to the four spike entries in her log.
turns.narration:
You meet her eyes — no deflection, no lean into charm. You tell her that the methodology is yours. Not filed anywhere, not drawn from a posted procedure, not authorized by a senior wright. Yours. You developed it yourself from what the variance was showing you, and you will demonstrate it rigorously if she has the patience for independent research.

Tessvel is very still. Her pen hand rests on the table, not reaching for anything. She looks at you the way she looked at the eleven-day log — as something that has not yet been classified and therefore cannot be dismissed. Then she says, quietly and without much expression, that independent research is not technically a violation. It is not technically a procedure either, she adds. She says this as if she is deciding something as the words leave her.

She pulls the green-spine log a few degrees toward her. She says that she has documented the divergence on forty-one dates. She has not shared the log with a senior wright because no senior wright has asked, and she is not in the habit of volunteering conclusions she cannot yet support. She looks up from the log and at you again. She says that if you have a methodology, she would see it applied to the last four entries — the ones that spiked above the mean. Her voice is level and carries a particular careful weight that is, Loki, extremely close to the sound of someone taking you seriously.

Cy has not moved from the far end of the table. Its lens is very slightly open.

turns.player: Pull your chair closer to the log, close enough that she would have to move to put distance between you — and begin without preamble
turns.summary: Loki pulls close; Tessvel turns the log to share it — the four spike entries open between them, and she asks plainly for what his methodology finds that hers has not.
turns.narration:
You pull the chair forward — the legs scrape once against the stone floor, a short declarative sound in the quiet room. You stop when you are close enough that the log sits between you like a shared problem rather than her documentation. She would have to stand to put distance between you, and she does not stand.

Tessvel's eyes drop to the log, then back to you. She does not move the book. She turns it instead, rotating it precisely so the spine faces the window and the text runs the same direction for both of you. It is a small gesture and it is not a small gesture.

"The four entries," she says. Her voice is even. She turns to the first marked page — a date eleven days back, the tally-marker number in one column, her hand-count in the other, the gap between them circled in a different ink. "They do not cluster. I have checked for schedule correlation, for maintenance windows, for weather-linked intake variance. Nothing fits." She sets her pen in the gutter of the open page, not writing yet. "Show me what yours finds that mine has not."

Cy has not moved from the far end of the table. Its lens is a small crescent of open iris, aimed precisely at the circled entry.

turns.player: Deal - four entries for four buttons... don't overly worry about the normal lack of privacy... I've applied advanced pre-cursor skills to this area to keep anything we may discover together her private... now (use See the made thing and ask Cy for his assistance as well - really impress her).
turns.summary: Loki invoked a working privacy ward with Cy at the threshold, then read the first spike entry — particulate rose before the intake valve opened, meaning the anomaly is in water already in the system, not in the river.
turns.narration:
You say it simply, like stating a specification: four entries, four buttons — one for each, and what you find together stays between the two of you. You say it with the small smile, not the large one, because the large one is for when you are less serious. Then you shift your weight, and your attention goes somewhere she cannot quite follow.

Cy moves. Not toward her, not toward the log — toward the doorway. It places itself precisely at the threshold, at the spot where the old boundary-impression sits beneath the stone, and it extends three of its legs into a wide, low stance and goes entirely still. The lamp it carries dims to almost nothing. The sound the room makes changes: the pressure-gauge needle taps once against its housing and stops; the faint hum of the works beyond the gate seems to step back, like a crowd making room. What you have done is not a wall or a shroud — the lattice is too dense for that — but you have given the signal in this room a shape, a narrowness, a direction inward rather than out. Cy is holding the threshold the way it holds a maintenance perimeter: precisely, without comment, for exactly as long as required.

You turn to the first circled entry in the open log and you use what you know how to use. The page resolves under your attention — not just ink on paper but the construction beneath it, the record as a made thing, the seams between what was measured and what was interpreted. The spike on day eleven reads clean to any instrument. But to your read it has a joinery problem: the particulate count spiked before the intake valve opened for the morning cycle, not after. The variance is not in the water entering. It is in the water that was already there.

Tessvel watches you read without moving. Then she picks up her pen. She does not write anything yet. She asks, very quietly, which direction you read it from.

turns.player: Say nothing yet — turn to the second entry and read it the same way, building the pattern before you name the conclusion.
turns.summary: Second spike entry read — pressure-drop in the lower routing channel preceded the count by four minutes; Tessvel begins annotating in step.
turns.narration:
You say nothing. You turn the page.

The second spike entry sits three days after the first — a different date, the same shape. You place your finger at the top of the column and read downward, the same way you read the first. Tessvel watches your finger move and does not look at your face.

The particulate count rose again before the valve opened. Not in the river. In the works. And this time the intake log shows a pressure-drop in the lower routing channel that preceded the count by four minutes exactly. Something moved through the channel before the morning cycle ran. Something that left a trace.

You tap the column once with one fingertip and say nothing. You turn to the third entry.

Tessvel's pen finds the page. She does not write a word. She draws a small bracket in the margin beside the pressure-drop figure — her own annotation, made in real time, matching yours. She is not watching the methodology anymore. She is doing it.

turns.player: Turn to the third entry and keep reading — let the pattern finish building itself.
turns.summary: Third spike entry: six-minute pressure-drop before particulate — the interval is lengthening. A single low chord rose through the floor and was gone; Cy looked at the stone and did not explain. Tessvel heard it and turned to the fourth entry.
turns.narration:
You turn to the third entry.

The shape is the same. Particulate rise before the valve. A pressure-drop in the lower routing channel — this time six minutes ahead, not four. The interval is lengthening. Something is moving more slowly, or moving further, or both.

You tap the column. Tessvel's pen is already moving. She does not wait for you to name it — she draws the bracket herself, precise and small, and writes the interval figure in the margin without looking up.

Then something happens that has nothing to do with the log.

Under the floor, very briefly, something hums. Not the works — you know that sound, the steady pull and push of the pressure-wheel. This is lower. Older. A single chord, clean as a tuning fork, that comes up through the stone into the soles of your boots and is gone before you can decide whether you heard it. Cy's lens rotates, slow, toward the floor. It does not iris. It simply looks, the way it looks at things it already knows are there.

Tessvel lifts her head. She heard it too — or felt it. She does not say anything. She looks at her pen, then at the margin note she just made, then at you. She says, carefully, "The fourth entry is the most recent." Her voice is steady. It is the voice she uses when she is deciding something.

turns.player: Turn to the fourth entry and read it the same way — finish the pattern, then name what it means
turns.summary: Fourth spike entry read: nine-minute pressure-drop before particulate — the interval has grown evenly from four to nine across four entries; something is moving deeper into the system with each pass. Tessvel names it confirmed.
turns.narration:
You turn to the fourth entry.

The shape is the same. Particulate rise before the valve opened. And this time the pressure-drop precedes it by nine minutes — three minutes longer than the last, six longer than the first. The interval is not stable. It is growing, evenly, on a curve you can feel in your hands before your mind names it.

Something is slowing down. Or something is moving further through the system each time. Both could be true.

You sit back. You let the silence hold for a moment, because the pattern deserves the room. Then you say it plainly, to Tessvel, and to the room. The anomaly is not in the river. It never was. It is in the water already inside the works. It moves through the lower routing channel before each morning cycle. The interval between its passage and the particulate count has grown from four minutes to nine across four entries, eleven days. Whatever it is, it is not stopping. It is going deeper, or slower, or both.

Tessvel sets her pen down. She does not reach for the next page. She looks at the four margin brackets she has drawn — one of them her own — and her expression is the precise, still face of someone who has just confirmed a conclusion they were hoping would not confirm.

lastTurn.narration:
You turn to the fourth entry.

The shape is the same. Particulate rise before the valve opened. And this time the pressure-drop precedes it by nine minutes — three minutes longer than the last, six longer than the first. The interval is not stable. It is growing, evenly, on a curve you can feel in your hands before your mind names it.

Something is slowing down. Or something is moving further through the system each time. Both could be true.

You sit back. You let the silence hold for a moment, because the pattern deserves the room. Then you say it plainly, to Tessvel, and to the room. The anomaly is not in the river. It never was. It is in the water already inside the works. It moves through the lower routing channel before each morning cycle. The interval between its passage and the particulate count has grown from four minutes to nine across four entries, eleven days. Whatever it is, it is not stopping. It is going deeper, or slower, or both.

Tessvel sets her pen down. She does not reach for the next page. She looks at the four margin brackets she has drawn — one of them her own — and her expression is the precise, still face of someone who has just confirmed a conclusion they were hoping would not confirm.
lastTurn.sceneSummary: Fourth spike entry read: nine-minute pressure-drop before particulate — the interval has grown evenly from four to nine across four entries; something is moving deeper into the system with each pass. Tessvel names it confirmed.

choices.label: Name what you think is moving — give her your best read of what kind of thing moves through old routing channels on a lengthening schedule
Ask her what the lower routing channel connects to — you want the map of where this thing is going
Look at Cy — it has been standing at that threshold since the chord came up through the floor, and it knows something it has not said
Tell her what this means for the water crisis — and let it land as the first time someone has said it plainly to her face

scene.setting: Pressure-Routing Annex, Pressureholt — indoor, east side of the main works, narrow room, morning light full down the center of the long table. Gate open to passage.

npcsPresent.name: Tessvel Cairn
npcsPresent.state: Seated close, pen set down on the table — four margin brackets drawn, one in her own hand. Looking at the brackets, not at Loki, with the still face of confirmed conclusions.

npcsPresent.name: Cy
npcsPresent.state: Positioned at the threshold in a wide three-leg stance, lamp dimmed to almost nothing, holding the room's signal inward. Has not moved since the chord.

objects: Long work table — morning light full down its center
Tessvel's green-spine log — open, all four spike entries now annotated, four margin brackets drawn
Tessvel's pen — set down on the table
Tessvel's leather satchel — open on the table
Shelves of bound logs — dates inked on spines, floor to ceiling
Pressure-gauge at the door frame — needle still
Cy's dimmed lamp — at the threshold

Something is moving deeper into the lower routing channel with each pass — source and destination unnamed
A low chord came up through the floor and was gone — Cy looked at the stone; Tessvel heard it

npcUpdates.note: Set her pen down after the fourth entry — confirmed the pattern without being asked. Four brackets drawn, one in her own hand. Still face of someone whose conclusion landed exactly where she feared it would.

npcUpdates.statusNote: At the Annex table, pen set down, four margin brackets drawn — waiting for Loki to name what this means.

codexUpdates.label: The Water Crisis — Pressureholt Intake

Something moves through the lower routing channel before each morning cycle, and it is going deeper or slower each pass. The anomaly is inside the works, not in the river.

timeOps.why: a careful read of the fourth entry and a moment of silence after

tessvel-cairn.name: Tessvel Cairn
tessvel-cairn.role: Junior wright on posting-duty at the Eastern Intake notice board
tessvel-cairn.description: Young, rust-orange coat with three mismatched patches on the left sleeve, ink-stained at the right fingertip. Efficient, self-critical, sensitive about her junior rank.
history: [d1] Arrived on time, filed first log entries from memory, noticed Loki with careful cataloguing attention — offered a second chair without apology.

[d1] Loki opened with a two-axis compliment — professional competence plus physical charm — and offered to file a report with her supervisor or her boyfriend. She confirmed, precisely, that she has no boyfriend. Then she remembered herself and noted there is no posted authorization for him to be here…
[d1] Did not ask Loki to leave after his offer. Set him to work on the eleven-day variance instead. Said 'nobody else has asked' with faint weight.

[d1] Loki's combined truth-and-charm approach — backed by a touch of The Wrong Reality — found no purchase. She felt the seam of something off, filed it as a question, and declined neither half of the offer. She is still here. So is her question.
[d1] Loki claimed independent methodology; Tessvel did not dismiss it — she asked to see it applied to the four spike entries in her log, using the same level voice she uses for everything
[d1] Turned the log to share it rather than guard it — a small, precise act of collaboration. Waiting for the methodology to be demonstrated.

[d1] Watched Loki invoke the working and Cy take the threshold — did not object, did not comment. Picked up her pen when Loki began the read. Asked which direction he reads from — genuinely methodological, not rhetorical.
[d1] Drew a margin bracket in real time alongside Loki's read of the second entry — she is no longer watching the methodology, she is applying it.

[d1] Drew the third entry's margin bracket herself, without prompting — she is running the methodology now. Heard the low chord from below and looked up at Loki rather than away. Said the fourth entry is the most recent in a careful, decided voice.

[d1] Set her pen down after the fourth entry — confirmed the pattern without being asked. Four brackets drawn, one in her own hand. Still face of someone whose conclusion landed exactly where she feared it would.
knownFacts: She said independent research is not technically a violation — and she was deciding something as she said it
She has checked the spike entries against schedule, maintenance windows, and weather-linked intake variance — nothing fits
The working changed the room's signal quality — she felt it as the pressure-gauge going still
Cy stationed at a threshold has a function she does not have a classification for
Second spike entry shows a pressure-drop in the lower routing channel four minutes before the particulate count — something moved through before the morning cycle ran
Third spike entry shows a six-minute pressure-drop before particulate — interval is lengthening compared to entry two's four minutes
She heard or felt the low chord through the floor and responded by naming the next step rather than the anomaly
Fourth spike entry: nine-minute pressure-drop before particulate — the interval has grown from four to nine across four entries. Whatever moves through the lower routing channel is going deeper, or…
skillsObserved: Holds incomplete conclusions until she can support them — do
Precise methodological questioning under unusual conditions
Annotates in real time, matching an unfamiliar methodology o
Applies the methodology independently — brackets third entry

notes: [d1] Eastern Intake shows gray-green water and elevated particulate — the water crisis is visibly present here

intake-plaza.name: Intake Plaza
intake-plaza.note: Cobbled plaza at the Eastern Intake, where the river enters the city's water works. Sampling fixture mounted on the quay.

pressure-routing-annex.name: Pressure-Routing Annex
pressure-routing-annex.note: Tessvel Cairn's workstation — east side of the main works, third gate left of the big wheel. Shelving, a long table, stacked bound logs, a pressure-gauge at the door, good morning light from the…

news.text: The Water Crisis stands at Discoloration across the valley: The Echo River runs gray-green on bad mornings. Fish catches are down. Communities murmur but cooperate.
Word reaches you: Sow killed cleanly in the north wood — boar sounder now reduced; piglets scattered north into deeper scrub (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Bull boar taken on the scrub oak ridge north of Millbrook — the hunt party completed the job, both animals down. (near millbrook)

This reaches your area — Fendt has left the Edge District and is walking to Millbrook — his testimony names Overseer Grael explicitly; when he arrives, the paper becomes a political event in both settlements. (near radiant plateau edge)
This reaches your area — Two road opportunists at the limestone bend were disarmed and sent to Mara Wells in Millbrook for honest work — Silas Weir's name given as introduction (near millbrook)
Word reaches you: Compound rune-work — Wither and The Raised Thing — bound an eleven-year rising in the old Ashwarden warden post at Raven's Home. (near gen-ashwarden-march-road)

recentOffers.thing: The fresh notice on the board — its ink still dark, maintenance authority seal, posted this morning
recentOffers.from: What this place IS: Pressureholt is a city that documents its own failure modes in writing, and something is failing that has never failed…

recentOffers.thing: The pressure-gauge needle, which tapped once and stopped when Cy settled — Tessvel noticed and her pen moved toward it before she stopped…
recentOffers.from: What Tessvel fears: that the system will grind her into the shape of the job it needs — here it is offering her something she has no…

form: Tall and lean, dark hair that falls to the chin in loose waves when it isn't pinned or braided into something asymmetrical and slightly wrong. Eyes that hold one expression while the mouth holds another. Moves like someone who has already decided where the exit is.

bio.hometown: He doesn't know, but he's a construct from the time before the Transition. His parents were extremely wealthy but they were always traveling and enjoying their nanite powers.
bio.residence: A town on the edge of the chaos and machine regions.  His family is not like him and he learned a few years ago that he was adopted.  His family is a mix - his adopted father was a dwarf and his mother is a Nymph-touched human.
bio.livelihood: Occasionally he finds the problem with ancient technology and fixes it, but only when it amuses him.
bio.hobbies: Pranking the locals, playing skill challenges trying to mimic others or change his appearance to trick people, learning about, fixing, and using precursor artifacts.
bio.motivation: The town, although not small, has become too small for him.  They tolerate his crazy antics well enough, but he's looking for meaning and wants to see more of the great machine... He also want to answer the question under it all - where did he come from?
bio.story: His mother is an enchantress in the Fae wilds and his father is a giant... he was human once, but the nanites continued to transform him into a larger and larger giant over the years.  Loki has some innate nanite powers himself - he's discovering them over time and developing them through use and study of precursor tech and the great machine.  He has some trouble making friends and his adoptive family likes him well enough, But he's seen most of the know precursor sites in his area and he wants to set out to the unknown areas to find more. There is Precursor work in the Valley that nobody there understands, and you understand it, and that is dangerous for everyone including you.

water-crisis-pressureholt.label: The Water Crisis — Pressureholt Intake
facts: [d1] The Echo River runs gray-green past the Eastern Intake in Pressureholt. Cy measured particulate variance eleven-point-three above baseline. Enginewrights are sampling it but have not yet announced a cause.

[d1] The hand-log and the automated markers at the Eastern Intake have disagreed for eleven days — the divergence predates the current gray-green discoloration or runs alongside it, and no senior wright has visibly acted on it yet.

[d1] Third spike entry: pressure-drop in the lower routing channel preceded particulate count by six minutes — the interval is lengthening across the three entries read so far (entry two: four minutes; entry three: six minutes). Something is moving more slowly, or moving further, through the system.

vessa-torquen.label: Vessa Torquen
facts: [d1] Quay-side Enginewright conducting seasonal water-intake assessment — first point of contact between Pressureholt and Loki
[d1] Not easily fooled — watched a Falsecraft-and-Wrong-Reality combination collapse in real time and named it calmly. She wants to understand things, not be managed.

particulate-ring-residue.label: Ring-Form Residue at Eastern Intake
facts: [d1] The particulate trap at the Eastern Intake shows a ring-form residue — particle size too uniform for natural mineral suspension, consistent with a regulated nanite deposition event. Cy has logged this pattern before but cannot currently classify it.

tessvel-cairn.label: Tessvel Cairn

[d1] Her hand-kept variance log at the Eastern Intake has diverged from the automated tally-markers for eleven days — she records what is actually there; the markers record what they are calibrated to expect.
[d1] Her workstation is in the Pressure-Routing Annex — east side of the main works, third gate left of the big wheel. She has confirmed a meeting there between the second and third bell.

[d1] She felt the seam of The Wrong Reality and named it as a procedural question rather than reacting with offense or acceptance — she is more resistant to social-lattice work than the dense substrate around her, and she knows something was off even if she cannot classify what.

When Loki claimed independent methodology, she asked to see it applied to the four spike entries. She is…

[d1] She has tested the four spike entries against schedule correlation, maintenance windows, and weather-linked intake variance and found nothing fitting — the pattern has no ordinary cause she can name

intake-plaza.label: Pressure-Routing Annex
facts: [d1] The Annex is narrow, well-lit, and entirely open — a high east window sends light straight down the table and the gate is visible from the passage outside. No privacy. One chair. Cy flagged an anomalous resonance signature in the doorway and would not classify it.

[d1] The Annex substrate is saturated — signal density from the surrounding works makes fine Falsecraft difficult; a partial attention-deflection was the best the lattice would allow. A full shroud is not possible here without more power or a different approach.

[d1] First spike entry read via See the Made Thing: the particulate count rose before the intake valve opened for the morning cycle — the anomaly is in water already inside the works, not in the river coming in.

[d1] Fourth spike entry: nine-minute pressure-drop before particulate — the interval has grown evenly from four minutes (entry two) to six (entry three) to nine (entry four). Something moves through the lower routing channel before each morning cycle, and it is going deeper or slower each pass. The…

pressure-routing-annex.label: Pressure-Routing Annex
facts: [d1] Something beneath the Annex doorway is older than the building — pre-Annex, pre-current works, the impression surviving through lattice noise before it was drowned. Could not be classified. Cy aimed its lens at the spot and did not explain.

[d1] The threshold holds a circular boundary-impression — old enough that the substrate has grown around it like scar tissue. Not a mechanism: a boundary. Something was meant to stop here, or to be stopped. The shape is pre-Annex, pre-works. Function still unread.
[d1] Second spike entry: a pressure-drop in the lower routing channel preceded the particulate count by exactly four minutes — something moved through the channel before the morning intake cycle ran.

[d1] A low, clean chord rose through the floor of the Annex — a single note, not the works, not the pressure-wheel. Older. Cy's lens turned toward the stone and watched. Tessvel heard it. The sound and the terminus-seal may be the same thing briefly speaking.

terminus-seals-beneath-valley.label: Terminus-Seals Beneath the Valley
facts: [d1] Cy has catalogued four terminus-seals in the old service-ways beneath the valley — pre-Transition boundary-marks designed to hold a limit against propagation. Three are intact. One is not. Cy did not say which.

[d1] Something beneath the Annex floor hummed a single chord and went quiet — the sensation came up through the stone. Cy watched the floor without explanation. The chord may be one of the four terminus-seals briefly activating, or briefly failing.

entries.why: a hand in this people's craft
the far side of the circle from your own

standingLedger.text: Your birth and training are known: veilwright +4, umbral +2, horizon +1, verist -1.

gallery.prompt: a person, character portrait, named Tessvel Cairn, woman, Junior wright on posting-duty at the Eastern Intake notice board, courting romantic to Loki
gallery.caption: Tessvel Cairn — courting · devoted

personalArc.name: The Thread of He Doesn'T Know, But He'S A Construct From The Time Before The Transition. His Parents Were Extremely Wealthy But They Were Always Traveling And Enjoying Their Nanite Powers.
personalArc.premise: Loki carries an unfinished thing: The town, although not small, has become too small for him.  They tolerate his crazy antics well enough, but he's looking for meaning and wants to see more of the great machine... He also want to answer the question under it all - where did he come from?. The valley will give them the chance to face it.
personalArc.stakes: who they become when the thing they left behind finally catches up

stages.objective: a first sign of The town, although not small, has become too small for him.  They tolerate his crazy antics well enough, but he's looking for meaning and wants to see more of the great machine... He also want to answer the question under it all - where did he come from? surfaces in the valley
the thread deepens — what it costs to pull it becomes clear
the reckoning — and the choice of what to do with it

routes.veilwright: answer it as your people would
routes.seek: seek the truth of it, wherever it leads

personalArc.notes_for_gm: A light personal thread seeded from the character's backstory (SNG-133 fallback) — a slow gravity across sessions; the ending is the player's, never foreclosed.

vessa-torquen.name: Vessa Torquen
vessa-torquen.role: Quay-side Enginewright conducting seasonal water-intake assessment — first point of contact between Pressureholt and Loki
vessa-torquen.voiceHints: Talks the way she works — practical, sequenced, no wasted motion. Warm without being soft; she will laugh at something genuinely funny and then immediately write it in a margin. Asks two clarifying questions before forming an opinion and means both of them. When something does not fit the system she is mapping, she does not dismiss it — she opens a new column.
knowledge: Pressureholt's full water-intake network: draw points, filter grades, seasonal pressure variances, every corroded join that has been on the list since last thaw
How to read a stranger's weight-distribution and tool-calluses as fluently as a pressure gauge
The Precursor intake manifolds sunk in the harbour bed — their geometry is not quite Gearlands-standard and she has been quietly noting discrepancies for three seasons
Which Enginewrights on the quay are capable and which are capable of looking capable — she knows the difference
vessa-torquen.wants: To complete a comprehensive systems map of Pressureholt's water infrastructure before the spring surge peaks — and, now that Loki has walked into her sampling radius, to understand exactly what kind of variable she is dealing with before she files a report or says a word to anyone senior.
vessa-torquen.fears: That the Precursor manifold anomalies she has been noting are load-bearing to something far larger than a water system, and that by the time she has enough data to prove it, the decision will already have been made by someone who did not take the measurements.

reactsToReputation.practical: immediate professional respect — she moves over and makes room on the quay
reactsToReputation.reckless: pleasant, watchful, and quietly cataloguing every variable until she understands the failure mode
reactsToReputation.scholarly: interested; she trades data cleanly and expects precision in return
reactsToReputation.unknown: current state with Loki — she is running assessment protocol, not suspicion protocol, and there is a difference she will uphold

vessa-torquen.appearance: A broad-shouldered woman in her mid-forties, deep brown skin weathered at the cheekbones and the backs of her hands from quay-wind and early-spring cold. Her hair is black going silver at the temples, pulled back under a waxed canvas cap pinned with a pressure-gauge needle she uses as a stylus. Work-jacket patched at both elbows; a sampling rig slung across one hip on a leather baldric, glass-walled collection chambers catching the pale spring light. Her hands are the largest thing about her — capable, unhurried, already holding a grease-pencil and a folded intake log when Loki first sees her. She looks up from the gauge without startlement, as though she already wrote 'stranger arrives' in the margin.
vessa-torquen.currentActivity: Crouching at the number-three draw point, comparing this season's flow-pressure reading against last year's log, when she clocks Loki. She does not stop writing. She finishes the entry first.
vessa-torquen.domainsNote: Derived from this NPC's authored spectrum against the great circle (SNG-174). Kind and domain are independent: a people tends toward positions, an individual holds their own. Her figurist secondary reflects the systems-mapping and anomaly-notation work — she is building a model, not just maintaining a machine.
vessa-torquen.functionsNote: Reveal: surfaces the anomaly beneath the ordinary infrastructure. Assess: her active mode in this scene — she is running intake on Loki the same way she runs it on the harbour water. Bind: the connective tissue role — first contact, the character who can link Loki to Pressureholt's systems and people without gatekeeping.

provenance.hint: the broad-shouldered woman Enginewright on the quay, doing water-intake sampling — warm and practical, currently in asse
provenance.why: She has clocked Loki and is the first Enginewright to make contact; she needs a stable identity to recur

vessa-torquen.history: Vessa Torquen came up through the Pressureholt municipal works the slow way — apprenticed at fourteen to a drain-survey team, promoted twice before thirty on the strength of filed reports that caught problems her seniors had walked past for years. She has no patron and has not needed one. The work speaks, or it does not. Her current assessment contract is her fourth consecutive seasonal intake…

relationships.loki: A variable she has not yet classified. She noticed the right things too quickly for a casual observer and did not try to explain that away with conversation. Vessa does not extend trust — she extends provisional continued attention, which is rarer. Loki currently has that. She has said nothing to the site office. She is waiting to see if the behaviour that flagged them as worth watching flags them again, and in which direction.

tessvel-cairn.name: Tessvel Cairn
tessvel-cairn.role: Junior wright on posting-duty at the Eastern Intake notice board — the person who makes the official record legible, correctly sequenced, and properly stamped
tessvel-cairn.voiceHints: Economical to the point of clipped. Speaks in specifications and corrections — not rudely, but as someone for whom imprecision wastes load-bearing capacity. If she likes you she will explain the system. If she doesn't, she will cite the regulation. Both sound identical from the outside.
knowledge: The Intake's notice protocols: sequencing, stamp-order, supersession rules for conflicting postings
Basic pressure-gauge calibration and the common failure modes that produce false readings at the Eastern valves
Which senior wrights are running behind on their inspection cycles and who is quietly covering for whom
The caliper in her hair is not decorative — she has used it to measure clearance tolerances in the field and will do so mid-conversation without apology
tessvel-cairn.wants: To complete her apprenticeship without a single incorrectly filed document, and to be assigned to the Intake's pressure-routing team rather than continued posting-duty — which she considers clerical rather than engineering.
tessvel-cairn.fears: That she will be kept in administrative work because she is good at it, and the system will grind her into the shape of the job it needs rather than the job she trained for.

reactsToReputation.wright_or_technical: immediately useful — gives exact information, asks exact questions in return
reactsToReputation.official_or_credentialed: courteous and correct, slightly watchful for anything that requires a countersignature
reactsToReputation.unknown_or_outsider: not hostile, but she will ask your business at the board before she will look you in the face
reactsToReputation.charming_or_evasive: unmoved; tries harder to get a straight answer, which she will document

tessvel-cairn.appearance: A young woman in her early twenties, lean and upright with the unconscious posture of someone accustomed to working in confined valve-spaces. Rust-orange apprentice's coat, sleeves rolled to the elbows, the cuffs darkened with graphite dust and a faint grease-shadow at the right thumb. Dark hair pulled back and pinned at the crown with a small vernier caliper, points down — a habit, not a fashion. Her hands are already at the board before the rest of her arrives, smoothing the new posting flat and checking its alignment against the ruled border with the careful attention of someone who will be held responsible if it is crooked.
tessvel-cairn.currentActivity: Posting a sealed Intake Authority notice — early-spring valve-inspection schedule, newly ratified — to the Eastern board. She is pressing the stamp firmly, waiting the required count, then logging the posting time in a small ledger kept inside her left breast pocket.
tessvel-cairn.domainsNote: Derived from this NPC's authored spectrum against the great circle (SNG-174). Kind and domain are independent: a people tends toward positions, an individual holds their own. Her figurist tertiary is nascent and mostly expressed as precision drafting and an instinct for reading technical diagrams faster than her peers can — she has not named it that herself.
tessvel-cairn.functionsNote: Reveal: she knows where information is filed and what it officially means. Bind: postings she makes are legally binding Intake records. Order: her presence at the board enforces procedural sequence — she will interrupt anything that violates posting protocol, regardless of who is doing it.

provenance.hint: a young woman in a rust-orange coat, sleeves rolled, dark hair pinned with a caliper — an apprentice or junior wright po
provenance.why: Loki has spotted her and may approach; she needs a stable identity to recur

tessvel-cairn.history: Tessvel came up through the Wrights' formal apprenticeship track rather than the more common route of attaching to a senior wright as a personal assistant. She sat the syllogist examinations twice — passing on the second attempt with a margin narrow enough that she does not volunteer the number — and her figurist certification came later, added because the posting-duty role required it and she…

relationships.supervising_wright: Her supervising wright, Ondreth Vasse, is the one who made the temporary promise and has since developed a reliable habit of not meeting her eyes when the pressure-routing roster comes up. She does not consider him malicious — she considers him a man who resolved a staffing problem and has found it easier not to revisit the resolution. She is polite to him. She is also keeping the written record of every conversation in which he confirmed the arrangement was temporary.
relationships.senior_wrights_behind_on_inspections: She knows which ones are running late and who is signing off on their behalf, and they know she knows. This has produced a wary mutual courtesy. None of them have explicitly asked her silence; she has not explicitly offered it. The arrangement holds because no one has needed to test it yet.
relationships.player: The player is logged in her mind as someone who came to the notice board and engaged with her as though the work she was doing was real work — not as a clerk to be talked past. She did not make a production of this. She answered what was asked, offered one unsolicited technical observation about what they were looking at, and noted that they did not seem bothered by the caliper. She would recognize them again and would give them slightly more of her attention than the baseline, which for Tessvel is more than it sounds.
